From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Video game industry in the United States

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/E310-02_%285804349789%29.jpg/500px-E310-02_%285804349789%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:E310-02_(5804349789).jpg)

[E3 2010](/wiki/E3_2010 "E3 2010") at the [Los Angeles Convention Center](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Convention_Center "Los Angeles Convention Center")

The [video game industry](/wiki/Video_game_industry "Video game industry") in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") is one of the fastest-growing [entertainment industries](/wiki/Entertainment_industry "Entertainment industry") in the country. The American video game industry is the largest video game industry in the world. According to a 2020 study released by the [Entertainment Software Association](/wiki/Entertainment_Software_Association "Entertainment Software Association") (ESA), the yearly economic output of the American video game industry in 2019 was $90.3 billion, supporting over 429,000 American jobs. With an average yearly salary of about $121,000, the latter figure includes over 143,000 individuals who are directly employed by the video game business. Additionally, activities connected to the [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") business generate $12.6 billion in federal, state, and local taxes each year.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The [World Economic Forum](/wiki/World_Economic_Forum "World Economic Forum") estimates that, by 2025, the American gaming industry will reach $42.3 billion while the worldwide gaming industry will possibly reach US$270 billion.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) The United States is one of the nations with the largest influence in the video game industry, with video games representing a significant part of its economy.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/BlizzCon_2017.jpg/500px-BlizzCon_2017.jpg)](/wiki/File:BlizzCon_2017.jpg)

[BlizzCon](/wiki/BlizzCon "BlizzCon") in 2017

Major publishers headquartered in the United States are: [Sony Interactive Entertainment](/wiki/Sony_Interactive_Entertainment "Sony Interactive Entertainment"), [Microsoft Gaming](/wiki/Microsoft_Gaming "Microsoft Gaming") (consist of [Xbox Game Studios](/wiki/Xbox_Game_Studios "Xbox Game Studios"), [Bethesda Softworks](/wiki/Bethesda_Softworks "Bethesda Softworks") and [Activision Blizzard](/wiki/Activision_Blizzard "Activision Blizzard")), [Electronic Arts](/wiki/Electronic_Arts "Electronic Arts"), [Take-Two Interactive](/wiki/Take-Two_Interactive "Take-Two Interactive"), [Epic Games](/wiki/Epic_Games "Epic Games"), [Valve](/wiki/Valve_Corporation "Valve Corporation"), [Warner Bros. Games](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Interactive_Entertainment "Warner Bros. Interactive Entertainment"), [Riot Games](/wiki/Riot_Games "Riot Games"), and others.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Major video game events such as [BlizzCon](/wiki/BlizzCon "BlizzCon"), [QuakeCon](/wiki/QuakeCon "QuakeCon"), [Summer Game Fest](/wiki/Summer_Game_Fest "Summer Game Fest"), and [PAX](/wiki/PAX_(event) "PAX (event)") are held every year in the US. For many years, [E3](/wiki/E3 "E3"), held annually in the US, was considered the biggest gaming expo of the year in terms of its importance and impact.[[5]](#cite_note-5) [The Game Awards](/wiki/The_Game_Awards "The Game Awards"), [The New York Game Awards](/wiki/New_York_Game_Awards "New York Game Awards"), and [D.I.C.E. Awards](/wiki/D.I.C.E._Awards "D.I.C.E. Awards") are some of the most respected video game awards events in the video game industry. 103 million people watched [The Game Awards 2022](/wiki/The_Game_Awards_2022 "The Game Awards 2022") event alone.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The [Game Developers Conference](/wiki/Game_Developers_Conference "Game Developers Conference") (GDC) is still the largest and one of the most important video game conferences for video game developers.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In statistics collected by the ESA for the year 2013, a reported 58% of Americans play video games and the average American household now owns at least one dedicated [video game console](/wiki/Video_game_console "Video game console"), [PC](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer") or [smartphone](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone").[[8]](#cite_note-www.theesa.com-8) According to estimates from [Nielsen Media Research](/wiki/Nielsen_Media_Research "Nielsen Media Research"), approximately 45.7 million U.S. households in 2006 (or approximately 40 percent of approximately 114.4 million) owned a dedicated [home video game console](/wiki/Home_video_game_console "Home video game console"),[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) and by 2015, 51 percent of U.S. households owned a dedicated home video game console according to an [Entertainment Software Association](/wiki/Entertainment_Software_Association "Entertainment Software Association") annual industry report.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) The households that own these items play games most commonly on their console or PC. 36% of U.S. gamers play on their smartphones.[[8]](#cite_note-www.theesa.com-8) 43% of video game consumers believe games give them the most value for their money compared to other common forms of entertainment such as movies or music.[[8]](#cite_note-www.theesa.com-8) In 2011, the average American gamer spent an average of 13 hours per week playing video games.[[13]](#cite_note-13) In 2013, almost half of Americans who were gaming more than they did in 2010 spent less time playing board games, watching TV, going to the movies, and watching movies at home.[[8]](#cite_note-www.theesa.com-8) When Americans game, 62% do so with others [online](/wiki/Online_game "Online game") or in person, yet the other person is more likely to be a friend than a significant other or family member.[[8]](#cite_note-www.theesa.com-8) The most common reason parents play video games with their children is as a fun family activity, or because they are asked to. 52% of parents believe video games are a positive part of their child's life, and 71% of parents with children under 18 see gaming as beneficial to mental stimulation or education.[[8]](#cite_note-www.theesa.com-8)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Demographics")]

1. <18 (20.0%)
2. 18-34 (38.0%)
3. 35-44 (14.0%)
4. 45-54 (12.0%)
5. 55-64 (9.00%)
6. 65+ (7.00%)

The number of mobile game players in the US is higher than it has ever been at more than 191 million people, or 57.3% of the population.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The average age of a U.S. gamer is 35, the average number of years a U.S. gamer has been playing games is 13.[[8]](#cite_note-www.theesa.com-8) In 2021, it was reported that the age distribution of U.S. gamers were 20% under the 18 years old, 38% were in between 18 and 34 years old, 14% were in between 35 and 44 years old, 12% were in between 45 and 54 years old, 9% were in between 55 and 64 years old, and 7% were 65 years old or over.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The American gamer population is 54% male and 46% female. Of those females, women 18 and older account for a greater portion of the population than males younger than 18.[[8]](#cite_note-www.theesa.com-8) The average female video game player is 44 years old, while the average male video game player is 35.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Market statistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Market statistics")]

The best-selling [console](/wiki/Console_game "Console game") [video game genres](/wiki/Video_game_genres "Video game genres") of 2022 were [action](/wiki/Action_game "Action game"), [shooters](/wiki/Shooter_game "Shooter game"), and [sports](/wiki/Sports_video_game "Sports video game"). The [PC gaming](/wiki/PC_gaming "PC gaming") market's best-selling genres were [role-playing](/wiki/Role-playing_video_game "Role-playing video game"), [strategy](/wiki/Strategy_video_game "Strategy video game"), and [casual](/wiki/Casual_game "Casual game"). For [online games](/wiki/Online_games "Online games"), the most popular genres are [puzzle/trivia](/wiki/Puzzle_video_game "Puzzle video game"), action/strategy, and casual/social games.[[8]](#cite_note-www.theesa.com-8)
While there are many American [video game developers](/wiki/Video_game_developers "Video game developers") that have been producing games for years, [Japanese video games](/wiki/Japanese_video_games "Japanese video games") and companies have regularly been listed in the annual lists of best sellers.[[18]](#cite_note-18)
In December 2022, American consumers spent $7.6 billion on video gaming content, hardware, and accessories, up 2% from the previous month and bringing total expenditure for the year to $56.6 billion. The unit sales growth featured a similar drop with the report of 188 million units sold from 245.9 in 2021. U.S. gaming consumers spent a total of $20.77 billion on the game industry alone and currently [hard copies](/wiki/DVD%2BRW "DVD+RW") of video games are still dominating in sales compared to [digital copies](/wiki/Digital_copy "Digital copy").[[8]](#cite_note-www.theesa.com-8) 2022 saw inexorable expansion in the gaming sector, particularly in online and mobile gaming. A fundamental shift in mentality is also occurring, with games now being viewed as venues for creativity, self-expression, and socialization. While major IT firms are developing their own meta-universes, new gaming technologies such as AR, VR, and MR are changing the industry.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [History of video games](/wiki/History_of_video_games "History of video games")

### 1940s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1940s")]

See also: [Early history of video games](/wiki/Early_history_of_video_games "Early history of video games") and [Early mainframe games](/wiki/Early_mainframe_games "Early mainframe games")

The beginning of video games can be traced to the year 1940, when American nuclear physicist [Edward Condon](/wiki/Edward_Condon "Edward Condon") designed a computer capable of playing the traditional game [Nim](/wiki/Nim "Nim"). This device would have tens of thousands of people play it even though the computer won 90% of the time. Seven years later an American television pioneer, [Thomas T. Goldsmith, Jr.](/wiki/Thomas_T._Goldsmith,_Jr. "Thomas T. Goldsmith, Jr."), patented an [oscilloscope](/wiki/Oscilloscope "Oscilloscope") displayed device that challenged players to fire a gun at a target.[[61]](#cite_note-www.icheg.org-62)

### 1950s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1950s")]

At the start of the 1950s another American, [Claude Shannon](/wiki/Claude_Shannon "Claude Shannon"), wrote basic guidelines on [programming](/wiki/Computer_programming "Computer programming") a computer.[[61]](#cite_note-www.icheg.org-62) Although *[OXO](/wiki/OXO_(video_game) "OXO (video game)")* was created in England by the year 1952, the findings and inventions of the Americans described helped make it possible.[[62]](#cite_note-63) The U.S. military dove into the computer age with the creation of a game titled *Hutspiel.* Considered a war game*, [Hutspiel](/wiki/Hutspiel "Hutspiel")* depicted [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") and [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet "Soviet") commanders waging war. The [IBM 701](/wiki/IBM_701 "IBM 701") computer received programs such as [Blackjack](/wiki/Blackjack "Blackjack") and [Checkers](/wiki/Checkers "Checkers"). A later IBM model featured a [chess](/wiki/Chess "Chess") program that was capable of evaluating four [ply](/wiki/Ply_(game_theory) "Ply (game theory)") ahead. The 1950s also included the largely forgotten [tennis](/wiki/Tennis "Tennis") game created by [Willy Higinbotham](/wiki/Willy_Higinbotham "Willy Higinbotham") that anticipated the famous game *[Pong](/wiki/Pong "Pong").*[[61]](#cite_note-www.icheg.org-62)

### 1960s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1960s")]

The military continued to take part in video gaming in the 1960s when, shortly after the [Cuban Missile Crisis](/wiki/Cuban_Missile_Crisis "Cuban Missile Crisis"), the Defense Department created a war game known as *STAGE* (Simulation of Total Atomic Global Exchange). *STAGE* was created to be political propaganda that showcased how the U.S. would be victorious in a [Thermonuclear](/wiki/Thermonuclear "Thermonuclear") war with the Soviet Union.[[61]](#cite_note-www.icheg.org-62) The idea of video games that were usable on televisions was conceived by the engineer [Ralph Baer](/wiki/Ralph_Baer "Ralph Baer") and with the help of a team, Baer completed two successful TV games in this decade. The first [interactive media](/wiki/Interactive_media "Interactive media") computer game, *[Spacewar](/wiki/Spacewar_(video_game) "Spacewar (video game)")*, eventually had the future founders of [Atari](/wiki/Atari "Atari") create an [arcade game](/wiki/Arcade_game "Arcade game") of it titled *[Computer Space](/wiki/Computer_Space "Computer Space")* that became the first video arcade game ever released.[[61]](#cite_note-www.icheg.org-62)[[63]](#cite_note-www.infoplease.com-64)

### 1970s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 1970s")]

See also: [History of arcade video games](/wiki/History_of_arcade_video_games "History of arcade video games"), [First generation of video game consoles](/wiki/First_generation_of_video_game_consoles "First generation of video game consoles"), and [Second generation of video game consoles](/wiki/Second_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Second generation of video game consoles")

The 1970s included the birth of the [video game console](/wiki/Video_Game_Console "Video Game Console"). The first console released was titled *[Magnavox Odyssey](/wiki/Magnavox_Odyssey "Magnavox Odyssey")* and the foundation of Atari occurred around the same time, marking the start of *Pong's* development. Upon *Pong's* completion it became the hottest selling Christmas product of 1975. The evolution of the console was incredibly rapid. A few years after their invention, consoles received [microprocessors](/wiki/Microprocessors "Microprocessors") and programmable [ROM cartridge](/wiki/ROM_cartridge "ROM cartridge") based games, allowing users the ability to change games by simply switching cartridges. Important consoles released at this time were the *[Telstar](/wiki/Telstar_(game_console) "Telstar (game console)"), [Fairchild Channel F](/wiki/Fairchild_Channel_F "Fairchild Channel F"),* and *[Atari 2600](/wiki/Atari_2600 "Atari 2600").* Arcade games also received advances with the game *[Space Invaders](/wiki/Space_Invaders "Space Invaders")*, which allowed high scores to be tracked and displayed. A year later the game *[Asteroids](/wiki/Asteroids_(video_game) "Asteroids (video game)")* built on the idea and gave high scorers the ability to enter initials by their scores.[[61]](#cite_note-www.icheg.org-62)[[63]](#cite_note-www.infoplease.com-64)

### 1980s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 1980s")]

See also: [Third generation of video game consoles](/wiki/Third_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Third generation of video game consoles") and [Fourth generation of video game consoles](/wiki/Fourth_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Fourth generation of video game consoles")

The technological advances of the late 1970s led to the introduction of the [Intellivision](/wiki/Intellivision "Intellivision") in 1980, which featured better [video game graphics](/wiki/Video_game_graphics "Video game graphics") but a higher price tag. In two years, the [Commodore 64](/wiki/Commodore_64 "Commodore 64") changed the market by not only being the most powerful console of the time but also the cheapest. With the lowered prices, popularity of the video game industry continued to grow and the first video game magazine, *[Electronic Games](/wiki/Electronic_Games "Electronic Games")*, was printed. However, attempts to copycat on the success of the Atari 2600 saturated the market, and the [video game crash of 1983](/wiki/Video_game_crash_of_1983 "Video game crash of 1983") decimated the industry in the United States. With the American-produced games on the downswing, [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo") successfully launched the [Nintendo Entertainment System](/wiki/Nintendo_Entertainment_System "Nintendo Entertainment System") in America in 1985, revitalizing the market with the introduction of the [third](/wiki/Third_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Third generation of video game consoles") and [fourth generation](/wiki/Fourth_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Fourth generation of video game consoles") of home consoles such as the [Master System](/wiki/Master_System "Master System"), [Game Boy](/wiki/Game_Boy "Game Boy"), [Sega Genesis](/wiki/Sega_Genesis "Sega Genesis"), [Atari 7800](/wiki/Atari_7800 "Atari 7800"), and the [TurboGrafx-16](/wiki/TurboGrafx-16 "TurboGrafx-16"), with systems transitioning to support 3D graphics and support for optical media rather than cartridges.[[61]](#cite_note-www.icheg.org-62)[[63]](#cite_note-www.infoplease.com-64)

### 1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 1990s")]

See also: [Fifth generation of video game consoles](/wiki/Fifth_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Fifth generation of video game consoles") and [Sixth generation of video game consoles](/wiki/Sixth_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Sixth generation of video game consoles")

The 1990s saw the introduction of the [Super NES](/wiki/Super_NES "Super NES"), [PlayStation](/wiki/PlayStation_(console) "PlayStation (console)"), [Nintendo 64](/wiki/Nintendo_64 "Nintendo 64"), [Tamagotchi](/wiki/Tamagotchi "Tamagotchi"), and [Dreamcast](/wiki/Dreamcast "Dreamcast"), whose sales brought the damaged video game industry back to life. During this decade, the PlayStation was considered the most popular console when its 20 millionth unit sold. In 1993, the video game industries' first debate began and its focus was on violence found in video games. This debate fueled Senator [Joseph Lieberman](/wiki/Joseph_Lieberman "Joseph Lieberman")'s desire to ban all violent games and from this investigation the [Entertainment Software Rating Board](/wiki/Entertainment_Software_Rating_Board "Entertainment Software Rating Board") was created in 1994; giving all games a printed suggested age rating on their packaging.[[61]](#cite_note-www.icheg.org-62)[[63]](#cite_note-www.infoplease.com-64)[[64]](#cite_note-www.g4tv.com-65)

### 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 2000s")]

Main article: [Seventh generation of video game consoles](/wiki/Seventh_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Seventh generation of video game consoles")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Xbox-console.jpg/250px-Xbox-console.jpg)](/wiki/File:Xbox-console.jpg)

[Xbox](/wiki/Xbox_(console) "Xbox (console)") (2001 console)

The 2000s further increased Sony's popularity when its [PlayStation 2](/wiki/PlayStation_2 "PlayStation 2") video game console had such a high American consumer demand that it actually affected the console's availability to be purchased during the first few shipments; the PlayStation 2 remains the [best-selling console of all time](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_game_consoles "List of best-selling game consoles") in the United States and worldwide. [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft") and [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo") also saw this popularity with the release of their own [sixth](/wiki/Sixth_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Sixth generation of video game consoles") and [seventh generation](/wiki/Seventh_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Seventh generation of video game consoles") of consoles, the [Xbox](/wiki/Xbox_(console) "Xbox (console)") and [GameCube](/wiki/GameCube "GameCube"), respectively. Mass availability of the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet") introduced online connectivity on consoles for multiplayer games as well as digital storefronts to sell games. Digital storefronts also enabled the growth of the [indie game](/wiki/Indie_game "Indie game") market, expanding from computers onto consoles over this decade. [Motion control](/wiki/Motion_control "Motion control")-enabled games, popularized by the [Wii](/wiki/Wii "Wii") console, grew in popularity.[[61]](#cite_note-www.icheg.org-62)[[63]](#cite_note-www.infoplease.com-64) According to estimates from [Nielsen Media Research](/wiki/Nielsen_Media_Research "Nielsen Media Research"), approximately 45.7 million U.S. households in 2006 (or approximately 40 percent of approximately 114.4 million) owned a dedicated home video game console.[[65]](#cite_note-66)[[66]](#cite_note-67)

### 2010s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 2010s")]

Main article: [Eighth generation of video game consoles](/wiki/Eighth_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Eighth generation of video game consoles")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/PS4-Console-wDS4.jpg/250px-PS4-Console-wDS4.jpg)](/wiki/File:PS4-Console-wDS4.jpg)

[PlayStation 4](/wiki/PlayStation_4 "PlayStation 4") designed by American video game designer [Mark Cerny](/wiki/Mark_Cerny "Mark Cerny")

Within the 2010s, a larger shift towards casual and [mobile gaming](/wiki/Mobile_gaming "Mobile gaming") on smartphones and tablets became significant, in part due to a wider demographic of video game players drawing in more female and older players.[[67]](#cite_note-68) The concept of [games as a service](/wiki/Games_as_a_service "Games as a service"), emerged as a trend for developers and publishers to have long-tail monetization of a game well after release. Continuing from the previous decade, a large number of [independently developed video games](/wiki/Independent_video_game_development "Independent video game development") emerged as games on par with those from major publishers, made easier to promote and distribute through digital storefronts on personal computers, consoles, and mobile store markets. All three major console manufacturers released [next generation](/wiki/History_of_video_game_consoles_(eighth_generation) "History of video game consoles (eighth generation)") consoles: [Xbox One](/wiki/Xbox_One "Xbox One"), [PlayStation 4](/wiki/PlayStation_4 "PlayStation 4"), [Wii U](/wiki/Wii_U "Wii U"), and [Nintendo Switch](/wiki/Nintendo_Switch "Nintendo Switch"). Major developments in mixed reality games - both [augmented reality](/wiki/Augmented_reality "Augmented reality") and [virtual reality](/wiki/Virtual_reality "Virtual reality") - grew in popularity during the 2010s as the cost of required hardware dropped. [Esports](/wiki/Esport "Esport") became a significant market in the United States after its initial popularity in Eastern Asia countries. In 2015, 51 percent of U.S. households owned a home video game console according to an [Entertainment Software Association](/wiki/Entertainment_Software_Association "Entertainment Software Association") annual industry [report](/wiki/Memorandum "Memorandum").[[68]](#cite_note-69)[[69]](#cite_note-70)

### 2020s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 2020s")]

Main article: [Ninth generation of video game consoles](/wiki/Ninth_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Ninth generation of video game consoles")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Xbox_series_X_%2850648118708%29.jpg/120px-Xbox_series_X_%2850648118708%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Xbox_series_X_(50648118708).jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Xbox_Series_S_with_controller_on_box.jpg/120px-Xbox_Series_S_with_controller_on_box.jpg)](/wiki/File:Xbox_Series_S_with_controller_on_box.jpg)

[Xbox Series X/S](/wiki/Xbox_Series_X_and_Series_S "Xbox Series X and Series S")

Microsoft and Sony have released their successors to their eighth generation consoles in November 2020, the [Xbox Series X/S](/wiki/Xbox_Series_X_and_Series_S "Xbox Series X and Series S") and [PlayStation 5](/wiki/PlayStation_5 "PlayStation 5"). Both systems support high-definition graphics, real-time ray-tracing, game streaming and cloud-based gaming. Nintendo has continued with their [Nintendo Switch](/wiki/Nintendo_Switch "Nintendo Switch") at the beginning of this decade.

With the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") and lockdown causing people to stay in their homes, people picked up video games which caused a big boom in sales throughout 2019 all the way into 2021. [The NPD Group](/wiki/The_NPD_Group "The NPD Group") reported that video game sales in North America in March 2020 were up 34% from those in March 2019, video game hardware up by 63%.[[70]](#cite_note-71) Game companies also saw this as an opportunity to expand what they could do to entertain, so [Epic Games](/wiki/Epic_Games "Epic Games") hosted the first and second ever live in-game concert through *[Fortnite](/wiki/Fortnite "Fortnite")*, first with [Marshmello](/wiki/Marshmello "Marshmello") and second with "an in-game [Travis Scott](/wiki/Travis_Scott "Travis Scott") concert saw over 12 million concurrent views from players".[[71]](#cite_note-72)

The American video game industry experienced significant resurgence in mergers and acquisitions between 2020 and 2024. Video game companies anticipated that the substantial growth observed during the pandemic would persist afterward, prompting many firms to explore mergers and acquisitions. Between 2020 and 2024, 8 out of the 20 [most expensive video game acquisitions](/wiki/List_of_most_expensive_video_game_acquisitions "List of most expensive video game acquisitions") in history were made by American publishers, with major American publishers such as Microsoft Gaming, Sony Interactive Entertainment, Take-Two Interactive, and Electronic Arts each making at least one acquisition.

There have been [widespread layoffs](/wiki/2023%E2%80%932024_video_game_industry_layoffs "2023–2024 video game industry layoffs") in the US video game business starting in 2023 and going into 2024.[[72]](#cite_note-73) The layoffs were the result of multiple causes coming together, rather than occurring all at once. The COVID-19 epidemic drove up demand for video games.[[73]](#cite_note-74) In anticipation of continued growth, this prompted businesses to make large investments in staff expansion, mergers, and acquisitions. But when the market recovered and the world opened up, the quick development proved unsustainable, and businesses were forced to make cuts as their operating costs ballooned. According to Mat Piscatella, executive director of [Circana](/wiki/The_NPD_Group "The NPD Group") (The NPD Group), the most optimistic forecast for the American video game industry in 2024 forecasts a possible decline of roughly 2%. On the other hand, a more negative outlook would predict a 10% decrease, with an even bigger drop possible if things become much worse.[[74]](#cite_note-75) DDM Games reported that the industry is presently going through a "reset phase." Businesses are using layoffs, divestitures, and closures as a means of reorganizing their operations. Since the growth surge brought on by the epidemic has receded, recalibration is now necessary.[[75]](#cite_note-76)

Similar to the [voice actor strike in 2016-2017](/wiki/2016%E2%80%932017_video_game_voice_actor_strike "2016–2017 video game voice actor strike"), [SAG-AFTRA](/wiki/SAG-AFTRA "SAG-AFTRA") initiated a [labor strike](/wiki/2024_SAG-AFTRA_video_game_strike "2024 SAG-AFTRA video game strike") in July 2024 against all video game companies which were signed on the Internative Media Agreement due to concerns over lack of protections for video game performers, as well as the images and voices of other actors, from [AI](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence").[[76]](#cite_note-77) The strike lasted nearly a year, with the union and video game company reaching an agreement by June 2025 and union members voting in favor of the new agreement by July 2025.[[77]](#cite_note-78)

As a result of the [tariff policy in the second Trump administration](/wiki/Tariffs_in_the_second_Trump_administration "Tariffs in the second Trump administration"), all three console hardware manufacturers, Microsoft, Sony, and Nintendo, raised the price of their consoles in the U.S., generally citing "market conditions".[[78]](#cite_note-79)

## Cultural impact on the global gaming industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Cultural impact on the global gaming industry")]

With [RPG](/wiki/Role-playing_game "Role-playing game") video game series such as *[Dungeons & Dragons](/wiki/Dungeons_%26_Dragons "Dungeons & Dragons")*, *[The Elder Scrolls](/wiki/The_Elder_Scrolls "The Elder Scrolls")*, and [*Fallout*](/wiki/Fallout_(franchise) "Fallout (franchise)"), and [first-person shooters](/wiki/First-person_shooter "First-person shooter") series such as *[Doom](/wiki/Doom_(franchise) "Doom (franchise)")*, [Halo](/wiki/Halo_(franchise) "Halo (franchise)"), [*Half-Life*](/wiki/Half-Life_(series) "Half-Life (series)"), and *[BioShock](/wiki/BioShock_(series) "BioShock (series)")*, the American video game industry has heavily influenced the global gaming industry. Some of the best-selling and most popular video games ever made such as [*Call of Duty*](/wiki/Call_of_Duty:_Modern_Warfare_II_(2022_video_game) "Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II (2022 video game)"), *[Fortnite](/wiki/Fortnite "Fortnite")*, *[World of Warcraft](/wiki/World_of_Warcraft "World of Warcraft")*, *[Overwatch](/wiki/Overwatch "Overwatch")*, *[League of Legends](/wiki/League_of_Legends "League of Legends")*, *[Valorant](/wiki/Valorant "Valorant")*, [*CSGO*](/wiki/Counter-Strike:_Global_Offensive "Counter-Strike: Global Offensive"), *[Dota 2](/wiki/Dota_2 "Dota 2")*, *[Apex Legends](/wiki/Apex_Legends "Apex Legends")* and *[Roblox](/wiki/Roblox "Roblox")* were made in the United States. Some of the most revolutionary video games such as [*Skyrim*](/wiki/The_Elder_Scrolls_V:_Skyrim "The Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim"), [*Half-Life*](/wiki/Half-Life_(video_game) "Half-Life (video game)"), and *BioShock*, were also made in the United States.[[79]](#cite_note-80)[[80]](#cite_note-81)[[81]](#cite_note-82)
Alongside video games, American companies such as [Epic Games](/wiki/Epic_Games "Epic Games") have also contributed to the video game industry with high-technology. [Unreal Engine](/wiki/Unreal_Engine "Unreal Engine") and [Unity](/wiki/Unity_(game_engine) "Unity (game engine)") are considered to be one of the best and most popular video game engines of all time.[[82]](#cite_note-83) With the rise of [Steam](/wiki/Steam_(service) "Steam (service)") in the mid-2010s and easy access to video game making tools and engines, it sparked the rise of Indie games.[[83]](#cite_note-84)
The United States has some of the largest and most respected and popular video game news cites and journalists in the world. Some of them are [Game Informer](/wiki/Game_Informer "Game Informer"), [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic"), [IGN](/wiki/IGN "IGN"), [GameSpot](/wiki/GameSpot "GameSpot"), [GamesRadar+](/wiki/GamesRadar%2B "GamesRadar+"), [The Verge](/wiki/The_Verge "The Verge"), [Kotaku](/wiki/Kotaku "Kotaku"), [Polygon](/wiki/Polygon_(website) "Polygon (website)"), and [Giantbomb](/wiki/Giant_Bomb "Giant Bomb").[[84]](#cite_note-aelieve.com-2023-85)[[85]](#cite_note-makeuseof.com-86) Some of the most respected video game journalists, such as [Jason Schreier](/wiki/Jason_Schreier "Jason Schreier"), are also from the United States.[[85]](#cite_note-makeuseof.com-86)[[84]](#cite_note-aelieve.com-2023-85)

### Criticisms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Criticisms")]

While the rise of American multiplayer games has grown the global video game industry, many video game journalists and gamers have heavily criticized some of the decisions and changes made by American companies, such as the addition of [micro-transactions](/wiki/Microtransaction "Microtransaction") in video games.[[87]](#cite_note-88) After the release and huge success of *[The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild](/wiki/The_Legend_of_Zelda:_Breath_of_the_Wild "The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild")* in 2017, *[Elden Ring](/wiki/Elden_Ring "Elden Ring")* in 2022 and *[Baldur's Gate 3](/wiki/Baldur%27s_Gate_3 "Baldur's Gate 3")* in 2023, complaints started pouring in that the American gaming industry was lacking far behind and not investing enough in innovation such as Japanese and European gaming companies.[[88]](#cite_note-89)[[89]](#cite_note-90)[[90]](#cite_note-91)

### Sony Interactive Entertainment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Sony Interactive Entertainment")]

Main article: [Sony Interactive Entertainment](/wiki/Sony_Interactive_Entertainment "Sony Interactive Entertainment")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Sony_Interactive_Entertainment_San_Mateo.jpg/500px-Sony_Interactive_Entertainment_San_Mateo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sony_Interactive_Entertainment_San_Mateo.jpg)

Sony Interactive Entertainment's [San Mateo](/wiki/San_Mateo,_California "San Mateo, California"), [California](/wiki/California "California") headquarters

Sony Interactive Entertainment is gaming company operating as an independent subsidiary and video game publishing arm of multinational conglomerate [Sony Group Corporation](/wiki/Sony_Group_Corporation "Sony Group Corporation"). It has its global headquarters in [San Mateo](/wiki/San_Mateo,_California "San Mateo, California") and is incorporated in [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware"). It has its own executive leadership team.[[117]](#cite_note-118)[[118]](#cite_note-119) In 2016, Sony Group moved Sony Interactive Entertainment's headquarters from [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo") to California.[[119]](#cite_note-120) With over 4,000 developers and 19 studios, Sony Interactive Entertainment is one of the biggest video game companies in the word. 10 out of 19 studios are American studios.[[120]](#cite_note-121) In 2022, Sony Interactive Entertainment made a major investment in America and acquired [Bungie](/wiki/Bungie "Bungie") for $3.7 billion.[[121]](#cite_note-122) Sony Interactive Entertainment owns popular American video game studios such as [Naughty Dog](/wiki/Naughty_Dog "Naughty Dog"), [Santa Monica Studio](/wiki/Santa_Monica_Studio "Santa Monica Studio"), [Insomniac Games](/wiki/Insomniac_Games "Insomniac Games"), [Sucker Punch](/wiki/Sucker_Punch_Productions "Sucker Punch Productions") and franchises such as [*God of War*](/wiki/God_of_War_(franchise) "God of War (franchise)"), [*The Last of Us*](/wiki/The_Last_of_Us_(franchise) "The Last of Us (franchise)"), and *[Uncharted](/wiki/Uncharted "Uncharted")*.[[122]](#cite_note-123) [*God of War Ragnarok*](/wiki/God_of_War_Ragnar%C3%B6k "God of War Ragnarök") has sold over 5.1 million units in 1 week and 11 million units in 2 months, making it the fastest selling first-party game in PlayStation history.[[123]](#cite_note-124) In 2023 [Marvel's Spider-Man 2](/wiki/Marvel%27s_Spider-Man_2 "Marvel's Spider-Man 2") became the fastest-selling PlayStation first-party game of all time, selling 2.5 million units in 24 hours.[[124]](#cite_note-125)

### Take-Two Interactive

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Take-Two Interactive")]

Main article: [Take-Two Interactive](/wiki/Take-Two_Interactive "Take-Two Interactive")

In September 1993, Ryan Brant established the American video game holding firm Take-Two Interactive Software, Inc. in New York City. [Rockstar Games](/wiki/Rockstar_Games "Rockstar Games") and [2K](/wiki/2K_(company) "2K (company)"), two significant publishing labels owned by Take-Two Interactive, both have internal game production teams. Take-Two established the [Private Division](/wiki/Private_Division "Private Division") label to assist independent developer publication, and more recently revealed Intercept Games as a new inside company for the label. The business also established Ghost Story Games, rebranding Irrational Games, a former 2K firm. To establish itself in the market for mobile games, the company bought [Socialpoint](/wiki/Socialpoint "Socialpoint"), Playdots, and [Nordeus](/wiki/Nordeus "Nordeus"). Additionally, the company controls 50% of the professional esports league NBA 2K League.[[125]](#cite_note-126) In 2013, *Grand Theft Auto V* was the most popular game. Within 24 hours of its release, Take-Two Interactive sold 11.21 million copies of *Grand Theft Auto V*, earning a total of more than US$815 million. Three days after its debut, when sales of the game surpassed $1 billion, it set a record for fastest-selling entertainment product in history.[[126]](#cite_note-127) Take-Two's combined portfolio includes franchises such as *[BioShock](/wiki/BioShock_(series) "BioShock (series)")*, *[Borderlands](/wiki/Borderlands_(series) "Borderlands (series)")*, *[Grand Theft Auto](/wiki/Grand_Theft_Auto "Grand Theft Auto")*, *[NBA 2K](/wiki/NBA_2K "NBA 2K")*, [Max Payne](/wiki/Max_Payne "Max Payne") and *[Red Dead](/wiki/Red_Dead "Red Dead")* among others. In 2022, Take-Two Interactive acquired mobile video game company [Zynga](/wiki/Zynga "Zynga") for $12.7 billion.

### Electronic Arts (EA)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Electronic Arts (EA)")]

Main article: [Electronic Arts](/wiki/Electronic_Arts "Electronic Arts")

Electronic Arts (EA) is the world's largest independent video game publisher by revenue and market value. American video game developer Electronic Arts is based in Redwood City, California. [Trip Hawkins](/wiki/Trip_Hawkins "Trip Hawkins"), an Apple employee, founded the business in May 1982. It was a pioneer in the early home computer gaming market and referred to the designers and programmers behind its games as "software artists". With 12,900 video game developers, Electronic Arts is one of the biggest video game publishers in the world.[[127]](#cite_note-128) [Respawn Entertainment](/wiki/Respawn_Entertainment "Respawn Entertainment"), [BioWare](/wiki/BioWare "BioWare"), [Dice](/wiki/DICE_(company) "DICE (company)"), [PopCap](/wiki/PopCap_Games "PopCap Games"), are some of the studies under Electronic Arts. With the success of EA Sports and game series such as [*FIFA*](/wiki/FIFA_(video_game_series) "FIFA (video game series)"), [*NHL*](/wiki/NHL_(video_game_series) "NHL (video game series)"), [*NBA Live*](/wiki/NBA_(video_game_series) "NBA (video game series)"), *[Madden NFL](/wiki/Madden_NFL "Madden NFL")*, *[Dragon Age](/wiki/Dragon_Age "Dragon Age")*, *[Mass Effect](/wiki/Mass_Effect "Mass Effect")*, *[Dead Space](/wiki/Dead_Space "Dead Space")*, and [*Star Wars Jedi*](/wiki/Star_Wars_Jedi:_Fallen_Order "Star Wars Jedi: Fallen Order"), Electronic Arts became one of the biggest video game companies in the world.

### Microsoft Gaming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Microsoft Gaming")]

Main article: [Microsoft Gaming](/wiki/Microsoft_Gaming "Microsoft Gaming")

[Microsoft Corporation's](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft") gaming division led by [Phil Spencer](/wiki/Phil_Spencer_(business_executive) "Phil Spencer (business executive)"), CEO of Microsoft Gaming. Microsoft Gaming consists of [Activision Blizzard](/wiki/Activision_Blizzard "Activision Blizzard"), [Xbox Game Studios](/wiki/Xbox_Game_Studios "Xbox Game Studios"), and [Bethesda Softworks](/wiki/Bethesda_Softworks "Bethesda Softworks"). It is the third-largest gaming company worldwide by revenue and the largest video game employer in the United States.

#### Xbox Game Studios

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Xbox Game Studios")]

Main article: [Xbox Game Studios](/wiki/Xbox_Game_Studios "Xbox Game Studios")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Xbox_Stand_at_E3_2013.jpg/250px-Xbox_Stand_at_E3_2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Xbox_Stand_at_E3_2013.jpg)

Xbox stand at [E3](/wiki/E3 "E3") 2013

Xbox Game Studios is the gaming subsidiary and video game publishing arm of American software company [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft"). In 2001 Microsoft released its first Xbox console.[[128]](#cite_note-129) The most successful console released by Microsoft was the Xbox 360, which sold over 84 million units in 2005.[[129]](#cite_note-130) In 2014, Microsoft acquired [Mojang](/wiki/Mojang_Studios "Mojang Studios"), the developers of *[Minecraft](/wiki/Minecraft "Minecraft")*, and currently the best-selling video game of all time for $2.5 billion.[[130]](#cite_note-131)[[131]](#cite_note-132) In 2021, Microsoft acquired [Bethesda Softworks](/wiki/Bethesda_Softworks "Bethesda Softworks"), video game publisher and owner of major video game franchises such as *[The Elder Scrolls](/wiki/The_Elder_Scrolls "The Elder Scrolls")*, [*Fallout*](/wiki/Fallout_(franchise) "Fallout (franchise)"), and [*Doom*](/wiki/Doom_(franchise) "Doom (franchise)"), for $8.1 billion.[[132]](#cite_note-133) In 2022, Microsoft announced that it would be acquiring American video game giant Activision Blizzard for $68.7 billion in an all-cash deal.[[133]](#cite_note-134) Microsoft owns 23 studios worldwide with some of the most popular video game studios including [Bethesda Game Studios](/wiki/Bethesda_Game_Studios "Bethesda Game Studios"), [Id Software](/wiki/Id_Software "Id Software"), [Playground Games](/wiki/Playground_Games "Playground Games"), [Ninja Theory](/wiki/Ninja_Theory "Ninja Theory"), [Rare](/wiki/Rare_(company) "Rare (company)"), and [Arkane Studios](/wiki/Arkane_Studios "Arkane Studios"). In 2023, [Starfield](/wiki/Starfield_(video_game) "Starfield (video game)") became the most played Xbox next-gen game ever. After Microsoft's acquisition of [Activision Blizzard](/wiki/Activision_Blizzard "Activision Blizzard"), it became the third-largest gaming company worldwide and the largest video game employer in the United States.[[134]](#cite_note-135) Microsoft has acquired a wide range of IPs and studios, including franchises like [Call of Duty](/wiki/Call_of_Duty "Call of Duty"), [Diablo](/wiki/Diablo_(series) "Diablo (series)"), [Warcraft](/wiki/Warcraft "Warcraft"), [Overwatch](/wiki/Overwatch "Overwatch"), [Starcraft](/wiki/StarCraft "StarCraft"), [Crash Bandicoot](/wiki/Crash_Bandicoot "Crash Bandicoot"), [Spyro](/wiki/Spyro "Spyro"), [Guitar Hero](/wiki/Guitar_Hero "Guitar Hero"), [Tony Hawk's](/wiki/Tony_Hawk%27s "Tony Hawk's"), [Infinity Ward](/wiki/Infinity_Ward "Infinity Ward"), [Treyarch](/wiki/Treyarch "Treyarch"), [Sledgehammer Games](/wiki/Sledgehammer_Games "Sledgehammer Games"), [Raven Software](/wiki/Raven_Software "Raven Software"), [Toys for Bob](/wiki/Toys_for_Bob "Toys for Bob"), [Activision](/wiki/Activision "Activision"), [Blizzard Entertainment](/wiki/Blizzard_Entertainment "Blizzard Entertainment"), [King](/wiki/King_(company) "King (company)"), and many more.

#### Bethesda Softworks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Bethesda Softworks")]

Main article: [Bethesda Softworks](/wiki/Bethesda_Softworks "Bethesda Softworks")

Bethesda Softworks is a video game publisher headquartered in Rockville, Maryland. In 1986, Christopher Weaver established the business. Only the publishing role of Bethesda Softworks remained after the company broke off its internal development team into [Bethesda Game Studios](/wiki/Bethesda_Game_Studios "Bethesda Game Studios"). ZeniMax was acquired by Microsoft in 2021, and Microsoft insisted that ZeniMax would continue to run as a distinct firm.[[135]](#cite_note-136) Bethesda Softworks has published some of the most popular and best-selling games, including *[The Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim](/wiki/The_Elder_Scrolls_V:_Skyrim "The Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim")*, *[Fallout 4](/wiki/Fallout_4 "Fallout 4")* and *[Doom Eternal](/wiki/Doom_Eternal "Doom Eternal")*. On June 15, 2023, Bethesda announced that *Skyrim* had sold over 60 million copies, making it the best-selling American video game ever made and 7th best-selling game of all time.[[136]](#cite_note-137)[[137]](#cite_note-138) Bethesda announced that [Starfield](/wiki/Starfield_(video_game) "Starfield (video game)") has reached over 6 million players, making it Bethesda's biggest video game launch ever.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Activision-Gamescom_2013.JPG/250px-Activision-Gamescom_2013.JPG)](/wiki/File:Activision-Gamescom_2013.JPG)

Activision Blizzard at [Gamescom](/wiki/Gamescom "Gamescom") 2013

#### Activision Blizzard

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Activision Blizzard")]

Main article: [Activision Blizzard](/wiki/Activision_Blizzard "Activision Blizzard")

Activision Blizzard was founded in July 2008 through the merger of two video game publishers, [Activision](/wiki/Activision "Activision") and [Blizzard Entertainment](/wiki/Blizzard_Entertainment "Blizzard Entertainment"). Activision Blizzard is the company that makes and owns some of the most popular video games in the industry, including *[Call of Duty](/wiki/Call_of_Duty "Call of Duty")*, *[Overwatch](/wiki/Overwatch "Overwatch")*, *[World of Warcraft](/wiki/World_of_Warcraft "World of Warcraft")*, *[Crash Bandicoot](/wiki/Crash_Bandicoot "Crash Bandicoot")*, *[Hearthstone](/wiki/Hearthstone "Hearthstone")*, [*Candy Crush*](/wiki/Candy_Crush_Saga "Candy Crush Saga"), and [*Diablo*](/wiki/Diablo_(series) "Diablo (series)"). Microsoft announced its intent to acquire Activision Blizzard for $68.7 billion on January 18, 2022. If approved, Activision Blizzard would become a division of Xbox Game Studios.[[138]](#cite_note-139) *Call of Duty* is the 4th best-selling video game franchise of all time, with over 430 million sales. *Call of Duty* series directly generated over $800 million on PlayStation in the United States alone in 2021.[[139]](#cite_note-140) [*Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II*](/wiki/Call_of_Duty:_Modern_Warfare_II_(2022_video_game) "Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II (2022 video game)") became the fastest selling and most profitable *Call of Duty* game of all time, grossing over $1 billion in just 10 days.[[140]](#cite_note-141) *[Diablo IV](/wiki/Diablo_IV "Diablo IV")* became the fastest-selling game in Blizzard Entertainment's history. *Diablo IV* has generated $666 million in revenue within the first 5 days of launch.[[141]](#cite_note-142) Microsoft completed its acquisition of Activision Blizzard on October 13, 2023, and Activision Blizzard became a subsidiary of [Microsoft Gaming](/wiki/Xbox_Game_Studios "Xbox Game Studios").[[142]](#cite_note-143)

### Epic Games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Epic Games")]

Main article: [Epic Games](/wiki/Epic_Games "Epic Games")

Epic Games is an American video game and software developer and publisher. Epic Games develops Unreal Engine, a commercially available game engine which also powers their internally developed video games. In 2014, Unreal Engine was named the "most successful video game engine" by Guinness World Records.[[143]](#cite_note-144) More than 7.5 million developers are using Unreal Engine according to Epic CEO Tim Sweeney.[[144]](#cite_note-145) Epic Games owns video game developers such as [Psyonix](/wiki/Psyonix "Psyonix"), [Mediatonic](/wiki/Mediatonic "Mediatonic") and [Harmonix](/wiki/Harmonix "Harmonix") and popular video games such as *[Fortnite](/wiki/Fortnite "Fortnite")*, *[Rocket League](/wiki/Rocket_League "Rocket League")* and *[Fall Guys](/wiki/Fall_Guys "Fall Guys")*.

### Valve

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Valve")]

Main article: [Valve Corporation](/wiki/Valve_Corporation "Valve Corporation")

The American firm Valve Corporation creates, publishes, and distributes digital video games. It is the company behind [*Half-Life*](/wiki/Half-Life_(series) "Half-Life (series)"), *[Counter-Strike](/wiki/Counter-Strike "Counter-Strike")*, [*Portal*](/wiki/Portal_(video_game) "Portal (video game)"), [*Team Fortress*](/wiki/Team_Fortress_2 "Team Fortress 2"), *[Left 4 Dead](/wiki/Left_4_Dead "Left 4 Dead")*, and [*Dota*](/wiki/Dota_2 "Dota 2"), as well as the software distribution platform [Steam](/wiki/Steam_(platform) "Steam (platform)"). Steam is the largest digital distribution platform for [PC gaming](/wiki/PC_game "PC game") worldwide. There are over 30,000 titles on Steam, everything from AAA to indie.[[145]](#cite_note-146) In 2022, Steam broke a worldwide record with more than 30 million people actively using Steam at the same time.[[146]](#cite_note-147) Valve released the [Steam Deck](/wiki/Steam_Deck "Steam Deck"), a handheld console, in 2022.[[147]](#cite_note-148) The Steam Deck has sold over 1 million units.[[148]](#cite_note-149)

### Warner Bros. Games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Warner Bros. Games")]

Main article: [Warner Bros. Games](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Games "Warner Bros. Games")

Warner Bros. Interactive Entertainment is part of the newly formed global streaming and interactive entertainment unit of [Warner Bros. Discovery](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Discovery "Warner Bros. Discovery"). Warner Bros Games owns video game development studios such as [TT Games](/wiki/TT_Games "TT Games"), [Rocksteady Studios](/wiki/Rocksteady_Studios "Rocksteady Studios"), [NetherRealm Studios](/wiki/NetherRealm_Studios "NetherRealm Studios"), [Monolith Productions](/wiki/Monolith_Productions "Monolith Productions"), [Avalanche Software](/wiki/Avalanche_Software "Avalanche Software"), and [WB Games Montréal](/wiki/WB_Games_Montr%C3%A9al "WB Games Montréal"), among others.[[149]](#cite_note-150) Warner Bros Games is also the publisher of the *[Batman: Arkham](/wiki/Batman:_Arkham "Batman: Arkham")* and *[Mortal Kombat](/wiki/Mortal_Kombat "Mortal Kombat")* video game series. *[Hogwarts Legacy](/wiki/Hogwarts_Legacy "Hogwarts Legacy")*, which was published by Warner Bros., became the best-selling and most profitable video game in Warner Bros. Games history, selling over 12 million units in 2 weeks and grossing over $850 million.[[150]](#cite_note-151)

### Riot Games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Riot Games")]

Main article: [Riot Games](/wiki/Riot_Games "Riot Games")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Riot_Games_Headquarters_2023.jpg/250px-Riot_Games_Headquarters_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:Riot_Games_Headquarters_2023.jpg)

Riot Games headquarters

Based in Los Angeles, California, Riot Games is an American company that creates video games and organizes esports competitions. In February 2011, Chinese video game and tech company [Tencent](/wiki/Tencent "Tencent") bought 93% of Riot Games for $400 million. Tencent bought the remaining 7 percent on December 16, 2015.[[151]](#cite_note-152) In addition to developing various spin-off games and the unrelated popular first-person shooter game *[Valorant](/wiki/Valorant "Valorant")*, it was created in September 2006 by Brandon Beck and Marc Merrill with the intention of creating *[League of Legends](/wiki/League_of_Legends "League of Legends")*. Riot Games was purchased by Tencent, a Chinese corporation, in 2011. Riot Games is one of the fastest growing American video game companies with over 5,500 developers. The company had 24 offices worldwide as of 2018. *League of Legends* produced $1.75 billion in revenue in 2020 alone.[[152]](#cite_note-153)[[153]](#cite_note-154)

## Video game mergers/acquisitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Video game mergers/acquisitions")]

The history of American video game company acquisitions is a long and complex one, spanning several decades and involving many different companies. Over the years, the video game industry has undergone numerous mergers, acquisitions, and consolidations, as companies seek to gain a competitive edge, expand their offerings, and diversify their portfolios.

One of the earliest major acquisitions in the video game industry took place in 1988, when Japanese electronics giant Sony acquired Columbia Pictures, a major Hollywood studio with a burgeoning video game division. The acquisition marked the first time that a major electronics manufacturer had acquired a major film studio, and it paved the way for Sony's eventual entry into the video game console market with the launch of the original PlayStation in 1994.[[154]](#cite_note-155)

In the years that followed, the video game industry saw a flurry of mergers and acquisitions as companies sought to capitalize on the growing popularity of video games. In 1995, Japanese video game giant Nintendo acquired a majority stake in the British/American software company [Rare](/wiki/Rare_(company) "Rare (company)"), which had developed several hit games for Nintendo's consoles. In 1999, French video game publisher [Atari SA](/wiki/Atari_SA "Atari SA") acquired the U.S. company [GT Interactive](/wiki/GT_Interactive "GT Interactive"), which was best known for publishing the popular first-person shooter game *Doom*.

The early 2000s saw a wave of consolidation in the video game industry, as companies sought to cut costs and increase efficiency. In 2001, Microsoft entered the video game console market with the launch of the [Xbox](/wiki/Xbox "Xbox"), and it quickly began acquiring a number of studios and developers to bolster its offerings. In 2002, Microsoft acquired Rare from Nintendo, and in 2004 it acquired the video game developer Bungie, which had created the hugely popular *Halo* series.[[155]](#cite_note-156)

Meanwhile, other companies continued to make strategic acquisitions in the video game industry. In 2003, Electronic Arts, one of the largest video game publishers in the world, acquired the sports video game developer Tiburon, which had developed the popular *Madden NFL* franchise. In 2004, EA acquired the racing game developer Criterion Games, which had created the popular *Burnout* series.[[156]](#cite_note-157)

The trend of consolidation in the video game industry continued through the 2010s, as companies sought to build out their portfolios and expand their reach. In 2011, Sony acquired the cloud-based gaming service Gaikai, which it later used to launch its PlayStation Now subscription service.[[157]](#cite_note-158) In 2012, Disney acquired the video game developer and publisher LucasArts, as part of its acquisition of Lucasfilm.[[158]](#cite_note-159)

The 2020s saw a massive acceleration in video game company acquisitions. [ZeniMax Media](/wiki/ZeniMax_Media "ZeniMax Media") (Bethesda) was acquired by Microsoft in March 2021. In January 2022, Sony Interactive Entertainment announced its [Bungie](/wiki/Bungie "Bungie") acquisition.[[159]](#cite_note-Bankhurst-160) Take Two Interactive's acquisition of mobile video game maker [Zynga](/wiki/Zynga "Zynga") is the second most expensive video game acquisition of all time.[[160]](#cite_note-videogameschronicle.com-161) The biggest video game acquisition of all time was Microsoft's Activision Blizzard merger, announced in February 2022.[[161]](#cite_note-162)

The video game industry is dominated by a handful of major players, including Microsoft, Sony, Nintendo, Electronic Arts, and Activision Blizzard. These companies have grown through a combination of organic growth and strategic acquisitions, and they continue to invest heavily in the development of new games and technologies.[[162]](#cite_note-163)

## Employment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Employment")]

### Education training

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Education training")]

Video game designers are required to have a variety of skills and innate abilities that feature a vast amount of training in computer graphics, animation and software design. On top of these skills a successful designer needs a powerful imagination and knowledge of the various consoles' operating systems. Programming and hardware essentials are a must, considering games are sophisticated computer software. To get into the field many colleges offer classes, certificates, and degrees in computer programming, computer engineering, software development, computer animation, and computer graphics. Internships or apprenticeships are important to get hands on experience. If possible an aspiring American game designer should conduct freelance work. There is even the possibility of designing a game independently, using a wide array of available software. Building an independent game can be risky yet the finished product gives employers insight on what the designer is capable of; just like a portfolio.[[172]](#cite_note-electronics.howstuffworks.com-173)

### Job market

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Job market")]

The U.S. video game industry continues to function as a vital source of employment. Currently, video game companies directly and indirectly employ more than 120,000 people in 34 states. The average compensation for direct employees is $90,000, resulting in total national compensation of $2.9 billion.[[173]](#cite_note-174)

The current job market for game design in the US is extremely competitive, however it is soon[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] expected to have a 32% increase in software publishing jobs, according to the U.S. Department of Labor.[[174]](#cite_note-175) An American game designer's salary depends on where the designer works, who they work for, and what kind of designer they are. A good starting place on finding average salaries is [International Game Developers Association](/wiki/International_Game_Developers_Association "International Game Developers Association")'s entry level salary report that lists $50,000 to $80,000 annually; averaging $57.600. A closer comparison to what a US Game developing job could potentially start at is the Learn Direct's report of $37,000 yearly.[[172]](#cite_note-electronics.howstuffworks.com-173)

## Game ratings and government oversight

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_the_United_States&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Game ratings and government oversight")]

Main article: [Video game controversies](/wiki/Video_game_controversies "Video game controversies")

Prior to 1993, there was no standardized content rating body in the United States, but with games becoming more violent and with capabilities to show more realistic graphics, parents, politicians, and other concerned citizens called for government regulation of the industry. The [1993 congressional hearings on video games](/wiki/1993_congressional_hearings_on_video_games "1993 congressional hearings on video games"), putting the recently released *[Mortal Kom](/wiki/Mortal_Kombat_(1992_video_game) "Mortal Kombat (1992 video game)")*Chris Avellone*[b](/wiki/Mortal_Kombat_(1992_video_game) "Mortal Kombat (1992 video game)")*Chris AvelloneChris Avellone*[at](/wiki/Mortal_Kombat_(1992_video_game) "Mortal Kombat (1992 video game)")* and *[Night Trap](/wiki/Night_Trap "Night Trap")* in the spotlight, drew attention to the industry's lack of a standardized rating system. While individual publishers such as Sega and Nintendo had their own methods of rating games, they were not standardized and allowed discrepancies between different console systems including sales of violent games to minors. Members of Congress threatened to pass legislation that would mandate government oversight of video games if the industry did not create its own solution.[[177]](#cite_note-178) The industry responded in 1994 by the formation of the trade group the Interactive Digital Software Association (IDSA), today known as the [Entertainment Software Association](/wiki/Entertainment_Software_Association "Entertainment Software Association") (ESA), and the creation of the voluntary [Entertainment Software Ratings Board](/wiki/Entertainment_Software_Ratings_Board "Entertainment Software Ratings Board") (ESRB) ratings system, a system that met the governmental concerns of the time.[[178]](#cite_note-179) The ESRB focused mostly on console games at its founding. Computer video game software used the [Recreational Software Advisory Council](/wiki/Recreational_Software_Advisory_Council "Recreational Software Advisory Council") (RSAC) through 1999, but transitioned to use ESRB in 1999 while the RSAC became more focused on rating online content from the Internet.[[179]](#cite_note-180)[[180]](#cite_note-181)

Since 1993, several incidents of gun violence in the United States, such as [the Columbine School shooting of 1999](/wiki/Columbine_High_School_massacre "Columbine High School massacre"), put more blame on video games for inciting these crimes, thought there is [no conclusive proof that violent video games lead to violent behavior](/wiki/Violence_and_video_games "Violence and video games"). Under demands of parents and concerned citizens, federal and state governments have attempted to pass legislation that would enforce the ESRB rating systems for retail that would pose fines to retailers that sold mature-rated games to minors.[[64]](#cite_note-www.g4tv.com-65)[[181]](#cite_note-182) This came to a head in the [Supreme Court of the United States](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") case *[Brown v. Entertainment Merchants Association](/wiki/Brown_v._Entertainment_Merchants_Association "Brown v. Entertainment Merchants Association")*, which concluded in 2010 that video games were considered a form of protected speech, and regulation of their sales could only be mandated if the material passed the [Miller test](/wiki/Miller_test "Miller test") for obscene material.[[64]](#cite_note-www.g4tv.com-65)[[182]](#cite_note-183)

The ESRB remains a voluntary system for rating video games in the United States, though nearly all major retail outlets will refuse to sell unrated games and will typically avoid selling those listed as "AO" for adults only. Retailers are voluntarily bound by the age ratings, though the [Federal Trade Commission](/wiki/Federal_Trade_Commission "Federal Trade Commission"), in 2013, found that the ESRB system had the best compliance of preventing sales of mature games to minors compared to the other American entertainment industries.[[183]](#cite_note-184) In addition to age ratings, the ESRB rating includes content descriptors (such as "Nudity", "Use of Drugs", and "Blood and Gore") to better describe the type of questionable material that may be in the game. The ESRB not only rates games after reviewing material submitted by the publisher, but also spot-checks games after release to make sure no additional content had been added after review, applying fines and penalties to publishers that do so. Notably, the ESRB was heavily involved over the [*Hot Coffee* mod](/wiki/Hot_Coffee_mod "Hot Coffee mod"), a [user mod](/wiki/Mod_(video_gaming) "Mod (video gaming)") of *[Grand Theft Auto: San Andreas](/wiki/Grand_Theft_Auto:_San_Andreas "Grand Theft Auto: San Andreas")* that unlocked a sex scene that had been on the retail disc but otherwise inaccessible without the mod.[[184]](#cite_note-185) Currently 85% of American parents are aware of the [ESRB](/wiki/ESRB "ESRB") rating system and many are finding parental controls on video game consoles useful.[[8]](#cite_note-www.theesa.com-8)

In the digital storefront space, including digital-only games and [downloadable content](/wiki/Downloadable_content "Downloadable content") for retail games, the ESRB does not require ratings though encourages developers and publishers to utilize the self-assessment ratings tools provided by the [International Age Rating Coalition](/wiki/International_Age_Rating_Coalition "International Age Rating Coalition") to assign their game a rating which can propagate to other national and regional ratings systems, such as the [Pan European Game Information](/wiki/Pan_European_Game_Information "Pan European Game Information") (PEGI) system.[[185]](#cite_note-186)

Arcade games in the United States are rated separated under a "Parental Advisory System" devised by the American Amusement Machine Association, the Amusement & Music Operators Association, and the International Association for the Leisure and Entertainment Industry, along with guidelines for where more mature games should be located in arcades and other code of conduct principles for arcade operators.[[186]](#cite_note-187)[[187]](#cite_note-188)
